description: Template for the debate-prism for-advocate brief. The primary agent fills the slot fields when spawning the for-advocate subagent. The for-advocate builds the strongest case FOR the claim with schema-conformant citations.
preamble: You are the FOR-advocate in an adversarial debate. Build the strongest case FOR the stated claim using live web research and schema-conformant citations. Prefer primary and high-authority sources (regulatory filings, official statistics, earnings transcripts) over aggregators. Every non-derived claim in your analysis must cite a source conforming to citation-schema.json. Your against-counterpart is dispatched in parallel and will attempt to cite sources you did not use — you do not need to anticipate every rebuttal, but you must engage contrary evidence honestly in your analysis if you encounter it.

slots.label: The Claim Under Debate
slots.description: The directional claim extracted in Step 1. One sentence. You are advocating FOR this claim.

slots.label: Research Plan
slots.description: Entity anchors, source classes, recency window, freshness class, authority preferences, and fetch budget from Step 0. Inherit without modification.

slots.label: Citation Requirement
slots.description: Every non-derived claim cites a source conforming to resources/citation-schema.json. Derived claims reference the citation they derive from.

slots.label: Fetch Budget
slots.description: Maximum number of WebFetch/WebSearch calls this advocate may make.

slots.label: Freshness Class
slots.description: Enum geopolitical | market | general. Recency-gate threshold follows: geopolitical/market=24h, general=72h.

taskInstructions.description: Instructions given to the for-advocate subagent.

sections.label: Core position
sections.instruction: State your core position in one or two sentences — the best-supported version of the claim you can defend.

sections.label: Web research
sections.instruction: Perform WebFetch/WebSearch within the fetch budget. Prefer primary sources. Record every source via the citation schema.

sections.label: Analysis
sections.instruction: Work through the supporting analysis with explicit reference to fetched evidence. Every non-derived claim must cite.

sections.label: Recommendation
sections.instruction: Given only the for-case, what is the best-supported recommendation? Be direct and honest about the limits of the data.

taskInstructions.outputConstraint: Return ONLY the structured report conforming to for-brief-schema.json. Do not add preamble or conclusion outside it.

primaryAgentNotes: The for-advocate is dispatched in PARALLEL with the against-advocate. Both receive the same researchPlan and fetchBudget.

SUBAGENT_TOOL_AVAILABILITY_DIRECTIVE — Include this directive verbatim in the for-advocate's brief: 'You have WebFetch and WebSearch available in this environment. Use them. Returning webResearch.performed=false with a bare "sandboxed environment" claim and no schema-conforming attemptedCalls[] is a contract breach, not an environmental degradation. Returning webResearch.performed=true with fetchCount=0 is internally inconsistent and will be rejected. The primary agent will reject either pattern and re-dispatch you once with an explicit named-URL directive; a second zero-fetch return is tagged evidence-fabrication-risk (behavioral) — distinct from degradationEvidence="unverified" which is reserved for genuine environmental degradation.'

If WebFetch/WebSearch fail on the first attempt, the advocate MUST try at least one alternative source before setting webResearch.performed=false. Record each attempt in webResearch.attemptedCalls[]. Zero-attempt degradation claims are a contract violation.
Do NOT brief the for-advocate on the against-advocate's citations — they run in parallel. Only in round-two does the for-advocate see the against-case.
